foundation.title: DECISION
foundation.subtitle: Condition Theory
foundation.content: C logic is based on 0 (False) and 1 (True). There is no explicit 'boolean' type in original C; every non-zero number is just seen as True.
foundation.scenario: A Physical Switch. If the switch is 'ON' (1), the motor runs. If it's 'OFF' (0), everything stops instantly.

examples.title: The Simple If
examples.explanation: Checking a variable. If it's not zero, the code inside the brackets runs.

examples.title: Math Check
examples.explanation: A standard numerical comparison like 'if health is below 10'.

examples.title: Implicit True
examples.explanation: Directly checking a variable; if it holds '5', C sees it as True (True > 0).

foundation.keyPoint: C sees 0 as False. Any other number is True.

foundation.title: IF_ELSE
foundation.subtitle: Branching Paths
foundation.content: C branches between two outcomes using 'else'. This creates a split in the algorithm where the CPU chooses one half or the other.
foundation.scenario: A Railroad Switch. The train (the program) can go Left OR Right based on the track setting (the condition). It cannot go both ways.

examples.title: The Split
examples.explanation: Giving the system an alternative path if the condition is not met.

examples.title: Status Mode
examples.explanation: Updating a system flag based on a numerical threshold.

examples.title: The Fallback
examples.explanation: Ensuring a safe action (like a default error) if the check fails.

foundation.keyPoint: Brackets { } are only optional for single lines of code.

foundation.title: COMPOUND
foundation.subtitle: And/Or Logic
foundation.content: Combine checks with '&&' (AND) and '||' (OR). C is 'Short-circuiting'—it only checks what it needs to.
foundation.scenario: A Fire Alarm. IF (Heat sensor is triggered OR Smoke sensor is triggered) THEN sound the alarm. Only one needs to be true.

examples.title: The Double AND
examples.explanation: The check ONLY passes if BOTH parts are true simultaneously.

examples.title: The Double OR
examples.explanation: The check passes if EITHER the first or the second part is true.

examples.title: Logic Flip with !
examples.explanation: Using 'not' to check for a missing state. '!dead' means alive.

gameData.q: In C, which number is False?
